serving.description: 1 bowl (~220g: 100g cooked taro balls + 100g cooked sweet potato balls + 20g brown sugar ginger syrup)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Jiufen-style taro and sweet potato balls weighs approximately 220g, comprising 100g cooked taro balls, 100g cooked sweet potato balls, and 20g brown sugar ginger syrup, providing approximately 326 kcal per serving.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Taro, cooked (NDB 11514); Sweet potato, cooked, boiled (NDB 11508); Tapioca, dry (NDB 20069); Brown sugar (NDB 19334); Ginger root, raw (NDB 11216)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA entry exists for the composite Taiwanese dish. Per-100g values are derived by weighted component estimation: ~45% cooked taro balls (taro + tapioca starch binder), ~45% cooked sweet potato balls (sweet potato + tapioca starch binder), ~10% brown sugar ginger syrup. USDA FoodData Central entries used for each component. FAO/INFOODS SEA consulted as secondary reference for taro and sweet potato composition in Southeast Asian preparations. Values represent the blended composite per 100g of the served dish.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of taro (Colocasia esculenta): GI ~54–56; Sweet potato, boiled: GI ~44–61; Tapioca/cassava starch: GI ~70; composite dish estimated GI ~65 based on weighted ingredient GI values
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for the composite Jiufen-style taro and sweet potato ball dish. GI estimated at ~65 using weighted component GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables: taro cooked GI ~55 (University of Sydney), sweet potato boiled GI ~61 (University of Sydney), tapioca starch GI ~70 (estimated from cassava starch data). Brown sugar syrup raises effective GI slightly. Composite weighted GI ~65 (medium category). GL per 220g serving = (65 × 35.0 × 2.20) / 100 = 50.05; however, using per-serving carbohydrate of 77g (35g/100g × 220g): GL = (65 × 77) / 100 = 50.05 ≈ 50. Reported as 22.75 per 100g basis for template field (GL per 100g = 65 × 35 / 100 = 22.75). Per-serving GL ~50, which is VERY HIGH.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (~20mg/100g; ~44mg per 220g serving); moderate potassium (220mg/100g) from taro and sweet potato
hypertension.guidance: Taro and sweet potato balls are naturally very low in sodium (~44mg per bowl) and provide potassium (~484mg per bowl) from root vegetables, making them a hypertension-friendly dessert choice when consumed in standard portions; no added salt is used in traditional preparation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycemic load (~50 per 220g bowl); high carbohydrate density (35g/100g, ~77g per bowl); tapioca starch binder and brown sugar syrup drive rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit consumption to half a bowl (~110g) or avoid entirely, as the very high glycemic load (~50 per standard bowl) from tapioca starch, taro, sweet potato, and brown sugar syrup poses significant risk of postprandial hyperglycaemia; pairing with a protein source or fibre-rich food may partially attenuate the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.1g/100g), zero cholesterol, zero trans fat; purple sweet potato anthocyanins provide antioxidant activity; high carbohydrate load may raise triglycerides with frequent consumption
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Taro and sweet potato balls are very low in saturated fat and cholesterol, posing minimal direct cardiovascular lipid risk; however, the high carbohydrate and sugar content (~77g carbohydrate per bowl) may contribute to elevated triglycerides if consumed frequently, and individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to half a bowl and monitor overall dietary carbohydrate intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Purple sweet potato anthocyanins (~40–80mg per bowl) with NF-κB inhibitory activity; ginger gingerols in syrup (~50–100mg) with COX-1/COX-2 inhibitory properties
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Purple sweet potato anthocyanins and ginger gingerols in the syrup provide sub-therapeutic but cumulative anti-inflammatory dietary support; zero cholesterol and very low saturated fat are consistent with an anti-inflammatory dietary pattern for rheumatoid arthritis management.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Taro and sweet potato balls (芋圓 yù yuán / 地瓜圓 dì guā yuán) in brown sugar ginger syrup is an iconic Taiwanese dessert most famously associated with Jiufen (九份), a historic hillside village in New Taipei City. The dish is prepared by mashing cooked taro or purple sweet potato, combining with tapioca starch (cassava starch) to form a dough, shaping into small balls, and boiling until they float and achieve a characteristic chewy, bouncy texture known in Taiwanese slang as 'QQ'. A standard bowl (~220g) contains approximately 100g cooked taro balls, 100g cooked sweet potato balls, and 20g brown sugar ginger syrup, providing approximately 326–345 kcal, 3.3g protein, 0.7g fat, and 77g carbohydrate. The dish is vegan, halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free. Nutritionally, it is very low in sodium (~44mg per bowl) and saturated fat (0.2g per bowl), but carries a very high glycemic load (~50 per bowl) due to the tapioca starch binder, root vegetable carbohydrates, and brown sugar syrup. Purple sweet potato provides anthocyanins (~40–80mg per bowl) with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. Ginger gingerols in the syrup offer sub-therapeutic COX-inhibitory activity. Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit to half a bowl. The dish is a quintessential Taiwan night market and tourist experience, served warm in winter and cold over shaved ice in summer. Source: USDA FoodData Central (component estimation); University of Sydney GI Database (GI estimation). Confidence: medium.

GI is estimated from analogous food GI values in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. FAO/INFOODS SEA consulted as secondary reference. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach and absence of a direct laboratory analysis of this specific dish.
